created_by: Wilderculture WILD Toolbox session (Claude) — fresh v3.2 build; supersedes the v2.0 'c1b7' record (c1b7 retired as a non-UKHab code)

ukhab_identity.composite_pk_note: v3.2: primary key is ukhab_level4 + wilderculture_subtype. UKHab classifies all sown grass/legume/herb leys (simple and multi-species alike) as c1b; this toolbox separates them as functional subtypes because their forage, palatability and ecological value differ. The sibling record is c1b + ryegrass_clover_ley.
ukhab_identity.secondary_codes_notes: UKHab has no standard primary or secondary code that specifically denotes a herbal/multi-species ley. Some applied registers differentiate c1b by management-intensity secondary codes (e.g. intensively vs less-intensively managed), but there is no bespoke 'herbal ley' code. The functional distinction is therefore carried by wilderculture_subtype, not by a UKHab code. (Flagged for review — see data_completeness.)

sources.title: UK Habitat Classification v2.01 (statutory classification underpinning the Biodiversity Metric)
sources.specific_claim: All sown grass/legume/herb leys, including multi-species/herbal leys, fall under c1b 'Temporary grass and clover leys'. Level 4 cropland codes under c1 are c1a, c1b, c1c, c1d, c1f. No code 'c1b7' exists; coding alternates letter/number so a trailing number would denote Level 5 (none defined for c1b).

sources.title: UKHab FAQs — coding structure (levels alternate letter/number, e.g. g3b5; secondary codes add detail)
sources.specific_claim: Levels 2–5 are coded with alternating letters and numbers; further detail is added via secondary codes, not new primary codes. Confirms c1b7 cannot be a Level 4 code.

sources.title: UKHab classes mapped in the Habitat Asset Register (c1b differentiated by management-intensity secondary codes)
sources.specific_claim: c1b is differentiated in practice by secondary codes (intensively vs less-intensively managed), not by suffixed primary codes — supporting the subtype approach.

nvc_communities: No NVC equivalent (sown multi-species agricultural sward); even less NVC-aligned than a simple ley
cross_references.nvc_communities_notes: Herbal/multi-species leys are engineered seed mixtures with no semi-natural NVC analogue. Recorded as none rather than forcing a fit.
cross_references.bng_metric_habitat_name: Cropland — Temporary grass and clover leys
cross_references.bng_metric_note: The Biodiversity Metric does not distinguish herbal leys from simple leys — both are c1b, low distinctiveness. The added ecological value of a herbal ley is real but not captured by BNG (a 'two values' case).

sources.title: Handbook for Phase 1 Habitat Survey (B4 Improved grassland)
sources.specific_claim: Agricultural leys map to Phase 1 B4. Standard reference, not independently re-verified this session.

ecology.description: A sown grassland containing at least three functional groups — grasses, legumes and herbs — typically six or more species. Grasses commonly include perennial ryegrass, timothy, cocksfoot and fescues; legumes white and red clover, sainfoin and birdsfoot trefoil; herbs chicory, plantain, yarrow and burnet. Deep-rooting components confer drought resilience and mineral access, and several legumes/herbs (sainfoin, birdsfoot trefoil, chicory) carry secondary compounds with anthelmintic (anti-parasitic) activity.
condition_indicators_good: All sown functional groups present and persisting — no single species dominating to exclusion of others
Deep-rooting herbs (chicory, plantain) maintained (>~15% cover each) and actively growing
Legume component strong (clovers/sainfoin) sustaining nitrogen supply and protein
Even multi-species structure with floral resource for pollinators
condition_indicators_poor: Herb species (chicory, plantain, sainfoin) declining or lost — sward reverting to grass-dominant
Aggressive grass dominating (>70% cover) — functional diversity collapsing (common under intensive grazing/high N)
Legumes lost — nitrogen and protein advantage gone
Docks, thistles or weed grasses establishing in gaps
ecosystem_functions: Forage production comparable to PRG/white-clover ley at substantially reduced nitrogen input; mid-season growth when ryegrass slumps
Biological nitrogen fixation via clovers/sainfoin (legume proportion can explain a large share of herbage N yield)
Drought resilience and mineral access via deep-rooting herbs
Anthelmintic forage — secondary compounds (condensed tannins in sainfoin/birdsfoot, sesquiterpene lactones in chicory) associated with reduced parasite burden
Pollinator nectar/pollen resource (chicory, clovers, sainfoin, birdsfoot trefoil)
Soil structure building via diverse rooting depths
ecology.whole_system_role: The regenerative production sward of choice in the Regenerate zone: high output at low N with soil, drought-resilience, animal-health and biodiversity co-benefits. Higher ecological value than a simple ley, though still a sown agricultural sward.

successional_neighbours.note: Loss of the herb and legume components reverts a herbal ley toward a grass-dominant simple ley; herbs are the least persistent group under intensive grazing.

sources.title: Multispecies swards outperform perennial ryegrass under intensive beef grazing (3-yr)
sources.specific_claim: MSS show potential for higher DM from lower N than ryegrass; legumes persist well but herbs do not under intensive rotational grazing.

ukhab_condition.condition_grade_note: Assigned per parcel. Descriptors below define the production-criteria scale for a herbal ley, framed on functional-group balance.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Excellent / optimal
condition_grade_descriptors.description: All functional groups present and balanced; herbs and legumes well represented; even, vigorous, diverse sward.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Good
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Productive with early signs of functional-group imbalance (some herb or legume decline).

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Moderate
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Grass beginning to dominate; herbs/legumes clearly reduced; diversity narrowing.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Poor
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Grass-dominant; herbs largely lost, legumes sparse; functioning as a simple grass sward.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Degraded / reclassify
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Multi-species character lost; reclassify (to the ryegrass_clover_ley subtype or g4).

sources.title: Toolbox of Multi-species Swards (TOMS)
sources.author_org: Duchy College Rural Business School / Rothamsted Research North Wyke
sources.specific_claim: Functional-group balance and persistence are the basis for assessing MSS condition; herbs are the least persistent group.

abiotic_template.parent_material_geology: Variable — sown on cultivable soil; deep-rooting components tolerate a wider range than a simple ley
abiotic_template.bioclimate_notes: Performs at lower nitrogen than a simple ley; high N actively suppresses the legume and herb components.

elemental_conditions.convention_note: REGENERATE TARGET STATE (Schema v3.2 convention): dials describe the productive target the layer aims for, not a current measured state.

water.descriptor: Target: aggregated soil holding moisture in structure; deep herb roots reach water in dry spells (drought resilience).

earth.descriptor: Target: well-structured, biologically active fertile topsoil; diverse rooting depths building structure.

air.descriptor: Target: well-aerated soil supporting root respiration and legume nitrogen fixation.

fire.descriptor: Target: high photosynthesis with growth spread across the season — legumes and herbs carry mid-season when ryegrass slows.

successional_envelope.current_stage: Sown productive multi-species sward held at an early, high-production stage (arrested succession)
range_of_natural_variability: Balanced multi-species ley maintained by appropriate grazing/cutting and low N
If grazed too hard / over-fertilised: herb loss, then legume loss, toward grass dominance
If abandoned: coarse grassland and scrub colonisation over time
successional_envelope.successional_pathway: Held by managed disturbance and low N. The characteristic decline is functional-group loss (herbs first, then legumes) toward a grass-dominant simple ley; the multi-species state does not self-maintain.

wild_assessment.energy_flow_role: Very high net primary production with greater seasonal spread than a simple ley, and a somewhat broader food web (pollinator support). Most energy is still harvested rather than cycled through a diverse community.
wild_assessment.wild_zone_rationale: A production sward whose purpose is high output at low nitrogen while building soil function, animal health and some biodiversity — the defining Regenerate aims. Treated to maintain functional-group balance, not protected or rewilded.
wild_assessment.disturbance_regime: Supplied — grazing/cutting and periodic reseed; the manager holds the dial. Grazing must be managed to let herbs persist (avoid over-tight residuals).

wild_management_target.detail: Maintain the multi-species target state: functional-group balance (grasses ~40–50%, legumes ~30%, herbs ~20%), legume-driven nitrogen supply, herb persistence. Keep nitrogen low (high N suppresses legumes/herbs). Re-grade trigger: two seasons measured a band above/below → re-grade.

wild_assessment.ecological_value_notes: Higher than a simple ley (grade 1): floral diversity, pollinator resource and structural diversity give real ecological value, though it remains a sown agricultural sward. Per the 'two values' principle this value is not captured by the BNG/UKHab condition descriptors and should be recognised separately.

rarity_irreplaceability.notes: Ubiquitous and fully replaceable; re-sown at will.

diversity.notes: Moderate — multi-species composition and pollinator resource, well above a monoculture ley but below semi-natural grassland.

energy_flow_contribution.notes: High, season-spread primary production with some pollinator support; trophic complexity still limited.

wild_assessment.naturalistic_lu_ha_note: Not applicable — Bioregionally Wilder field. c1b herbal ley is a Regenerate production sward.
wild_assessment.mosaic_role_note: Not applicable — a managed production sward, not a wilder-mosaic patch.
wild_assessment.preferential_grazing_magnet_note: False in context (a managed sward, not a mosaic component).
wild_assessment.process_indicators_note: Empty by design — Regenerate uses a state target.

sources.title: WILD Method Statement — The Science Behind WILD
sources.specific_claim: Framework basis for elemental dials, zone and the Regenerate target-state reading. Overlay layer, not L1–L3 core data.

forage.overall_palatability_notes: Highly palatable — chicory, plantain and legumes are well taken. Added animal-health benefit from anthelmintic compounds. Bloat risk lower than a high pure-clover sward but present with high legume content; manage introduction.

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: GROSS production, well-managed farm-scale herbal ley. CONTESTED magnitude — flag in client-facing use. Germinal 4-yr trial (industry, L6): 9-component MSS 47.1 t/4yr (~11.8 t/yr) vs PRG+clover 44.0 t/4yr (~11.0 t/yr) and PRG monoculture 35.4 t/4yr; very diverse (16-component) mixes yield ~6 t less. Defensible claim: comparable-to-modestly-higher yield than PRG/WC, clearly higher than PRG monoculture, at substantially lower N. Apply utilisation_factor for utilisable/LU.
Estimated — functional-group imbalance beginning to cost yield/quality.
Estimated — grass-dominant, behaving as a simple sward.

forage_yield_literature.contest_note: The MSS-vs-PRG yield comparison is actively debated. Vs PRG MONOCULTURE, MSS clearly outyield (e.g. Germinal +33% over 4 yr). Vs PRG+WHITE CLOVER the uplift is modest and mix-dependent (a 'sweet spot' around 6–9 components; very diverse mixes yield less). The robust, agreed finding (TOMS L2; BBSRC SARIC L1; Plant & Soil 2025 L1) is comparable yield at much lower nitrogen — the case rests on nitrogen-use efficiency, season-spread and drought resilience, not raw yield superiority. Both framings stored; not adjudicated.

sources.title: Toolbox of Multi-species Swards (TOMS)
sources.specific_claim: MSS matched PRG biomass at zero N vs PRG at 250 kg N/ha; most robust UK-specific MSS yield dataset.

sources.title: Multispecies swards improve nitrogen use efficiency and reduce nitrogen surplus
sources.specific_claim: 6- and 12-species swards (70 kg N/ha) outperformed PRG (170 kg N/ha) on NUE without compromising yield; legume proportion explained ~55% of herbage N yield.

sources.title: Multi-species swards outperform monocultures in trials (4-yr, Wiltshire)
sources.specific_claim: 4-yr totals: PRG 35.4 t; PRG+WC 44.0 t (+24%); 9-component MSS 47.1 t (+33%); 16-component ~6 t less than grass-clover. Higher N not cost-effective. Industry source — treat yield uplift cautiously.

utilisation_factor.notes: As for grassland generally (AHDB: set-stocked <50%; rotational 60–70%; paddock 75–80%). Slightly lower default than a simple ley at grade 1 because herbs persist better under less-tight residuals — chasing maximum utilisation costs the herb component. Ground-truth per farm.

sources.title: Utilisation is key to a successful grazing system; grazing cover targets
sources.specific_claim: Utilisation rises with correct covers/residuals; set-stocked <50%, rotational/paddock higher.

forage.conservation_lu_ha_note: Not applicable — Protect & Restore field. LU/ha for c1b is derived via gross DM × utilisation ÷ 5.5 t/LU.

nutritional_quality_by_grade.notes: ME broadly comparable to PRG/WC ley (Cooledge et al 2024, L1: no significant ME difference). CP highly variable and legume-dependent — leys with >30% legume run higher. Herbs slightly lower ME when mature/flowering; legumes compensate.
Estimated — behaving as a grass sward.

forage.mineral_status_note: CONTESTED — do not overstate. Cooledge et al 2024 (L1): significantly higher macro- and micronutrients (including copper) in herbal vs grass-clover ley, both seasons. TOMS 2022 (L2): forage minerals relatively similar between MSS and PRG/WC. Both stored; not adjudicated. Deep-rooting mineral access is a sound mechanism but the field evidence is mixed. Full mineral data sits in the Plant Species entity (Phase 2B).

nutritional_quality_sources.title: Cooledge et al. — nutritive value and minerals of herbal vs grass-clover leys
nutritional_quality_sources.specific_claim: No significant difference in ME or overall CP between herbal and grass-clover ley; significantly higher macro/micro-minerals (incl. Cu) in the herbal ley. Cited from the verified v2.0 sourcing; primary not re-fetched this session.

nutritional_quality_sources.title: Multi-species swards outperform monocultures (ME figures)
nutritional_quality_sources.specific_claim: 4-yr mean ME: PRG 10.69 MJ/kg; clover 11.49 MJ/kg.

seasonal_forage_curve.spring: Moderate — legumes/herbs slower to start than ryegrass; grass carries early season
seasonal_forage_curve.summer: High — legumes and herbs grow strongly when ryegrass slows (the season-spread advantage); drought resilience from deep roots

optimal_grazing_animals.notes: Rotational grazing; breed-level matching via the Animal entity.
Anthelmintic forage benefit (sainfoin/birdsfoot tannins, chicory) is most valuable for young stock.

unsuitable_animals.breed: Very hardy upland-only breeds (as sole use)
unsuitable_animals.reason: High-output regenerative ground is better deployed on stock that convert it; hardy breeds belong on semi-natural habitat.

timing_windows.preferred_season: Rotational grazing April–October; conservation cuts in the growing season
timing_windows.rationale: Match grazing to the season-spread growth; leave adequate residuals so herbs (chicory crowns) persist
timing_windows.avoid: Over-tight grazing and high nitrogen — both suppress the herb and legume components
timing_windows.per_species_notes: Introduce stock to high-legume swards gradually (bloat risk).

management.intensity_guidance: Stocking is SCHEME-TIED and never averaged. Derive LU/ha from utilised DM ÷ 5.5 t per LU: at grade 1, gross 10–13 t × ~0.75 utilisation ≈ 7.5–9.75 t utilised ≈ ~1.4–1.8 LU/ha as a planning figure. England: express in GLU (Natural England/ELM definition); Scotland: LU (SAC/FAS TN686). Take each from its own scheme — do not average.
management.monitoring_protocol: Plate meter / sward stick (pre-graze ~2,500–3,000 kg DM/ha; residual ~1,500 kg DM/ha but err looser to protect herbs); track functional-group balance (legume % and herb % as well as total cover); apply the re-grade trigger.

bng_unit_value.value_note: Low distinctiveness in the Statutory Biodiversity Metric (c1b cropland, same as a simple ley). The herbal ley's greater actual biodiversity value is not captured by BNG — record it via ecological_value_grade and value_axes instead.

sources.title: Statutory Biodiversity Metric
sources.specific_claim: Temporary leys (c1b) are low distinctiveness regardless of species mix. Exact current label to confirm against the live metric.

key_positive_indicators: Sainfoin (Onobrychis viciifolia) / birdsfoot trefoil (Lotus corniculatus) present
species_list.note: Skeleton only — full species records, anthelmintic secondary-compound data, and the Habitat↔Species join are built in Phase 2B. Sown mix, so the join is agronomic rather than NVC-constancy based.

fields_fully_populated: ukhab_identity — code corrected this session: c1b confirmed, 'c1b7' retired as a non-UKHab code; differentiated via wilderculture_subtype (v3.2 composite PK)
nutritional ME and the nitrogen-saving yield case (L1: Cooledge 2024, Plant & Soil 2025, BBSRC SARIC)
fields_partially_populated: forage_yield magnitude — CONTESTED (stored both framings, not adjudicated); grade 1 medium confidence; grades 3–5 ESTIMATED
crude protein — genuinely variable and legume-dependent (medium)
mineral status — CONTESTED (Cooledge 2024 higher incl. Cu vs TOMS similar); both stored, not adjudicated; full data in Phase 2B
wild_assessment.elemental_conditions — Regenerate TARGET-STATE dials, framework-reasoned (not measured)
secondary_codes — UKHab has no bespoke herbal-ley code; distinction carried by wilderculture_subtype (flagged for review)
fields_pending_research: Whether to attach a specific UKHab management-intensity secondary code to c1b records (review against the full UKHab v2.01 secondary-code list)
Re-fetch of the Cooledge et al 2024 primary (carried from verified v2.0 sourcing this session)
Anthelmintic/secondary-compound and mineral data per species (Plant Species entity, Phase 2B)
Per-farm ground-truthing of utilisation and derived LU/ha
data_completeness.notes: Identity is now correct and honest: one UKHab code (c1b) with two Wilderculture functional subtypes. Yield and mineral claims are contested and handled per the integrity rule (both sources stored, conflict noted, no adjudication). Gross-vs-utilised DM cleanly separated. This is the second of the c1b pair; the sibling ryegrass_clover_ley record is updated to v3.2 to match.

sources.title: Toolbox of Multi-species Swards (TOMS)
sources.specific_claim: UK-specific MSS yield/nutrition dataset; yield comparable at much lower N.

sources.title: Multispecies swards improve nitrogen use efficiency
sources.specific_claim: MSS outperform PRG on NUE at lower N without compromising yield.

sources.title: Cooledge et al. — herbal vs grass-clover ley nutritive value/minerals
sources.specific_claim: No sig ME/CP difference; higher minerals incl. Cu in herbal ley.
